name: Hindu Pralaya
shortDescription: Pralaya is a Sanskrit term for dissolution or cosmic deluge in Hindu cosmology, representing a periodic destruction and recreation of the universe.
description: Pralaya, in Hindu cosmology, refers to the periodic dissolution or destruction of the universe. It is a cyclical process, an integral part of the Hindu understanding of time and existence. Unlike a singular, final apocalypse, Pralaya is recurring, marking the end of a kalpa (a day of Brahma, lasting 4.32 billion human years) and preceding a new creation. The concept is closely tied to the idea of Brahma, the creator, Vishnu, the preserver, and Shiva, the destroyer, forming a cosmic trinity responsible for the cyclical nature of the universe. During Pralaya, the three lokas (earth, atmosphere, and heaven) are consumed by fire, water, and wind, eventually merging back into the primordial state of Prakriti (primordial substance). This period of dissolution is followed by a period of rest for Brahma before he begins the process of creation anew. Different types of Pralaya are described in Hindu scriptures, including Nitya Pralaya (daily dissolution during sleep), Avaantar Pralaya (dissolution at the end of Brahma's day), and Maha Pralaya (the great dissolution at the end of Brahma's life). The concept of Pralaya highlights the impermanence of existence and the cyclical nature of time, emphasizing the importance of detachment and spiritual liberation (moksha) from the cycle of birth and death.
